cw.home.title: CITYWALK WITH LOCAL STUDENTS
cw.home.description: Walk with a local. See Beijing like a friend lives here.
cw.home.tags.title: Three Tier Service Tags
cw.home.tags.item1.title: Introductory
cw.home.tags.item1.content: 2-3 hrs | Great for first-timers | City first impressions
cw.home.tags.item2.title: In-Depth
cw.home.tags.item2.content: Half day 4-5 hrs | Themed routes | Real local life
cw.home.tags.item3.title: Immersive
cw.home.tags.item3.content: Full day 6-8 hrs | Customisable | Deep cultural experience
cw.description.title: Service Short Description
cw.description.content: Citywalk with Local Students pairs you with a Beijing university student for a city walk designed around what you actually want to know — how people live in hutongs, where young Beijingers really go, which traditional crafts are quietly disappearing. No rehearsed commentary, no tourist-only routes. Just a local friend walking you through the city they know. All you need is curiosity.
cw.part1.title: 1. Service Background & Introduction
cw.part1.para1: Most visitors to Beijing end up with two options: join a standard guided tour — walk fast, listen to dates and dynasties, leave without talking to a single local — or go it alone with Google Maps and a vague feeling that something is missing. You see the hutong. You just don't know who lives there, or what their life looks like.
cw.part1.para2: <1>Citywalk with Local Students</1> was built to close that gap.
cw.part1.para3: We recruit Beijing university students as guides and ask them to share the city they actually live in — not a performance of it. Their favourite breakfast stall. The back alley their neighbourhood grew up in. The card game happening outside the corner shop every afternoon. These are the details that make Beijing feel real, and they only come from someone who is genuinely part of it.
cw.part1.listitem1: A real local friend — not a tour guide with a flag
cw.part1.listitem2: Chances to use Mandarin in actual situations, not language-class drills
cw.part1.listitem3: Routes that locals themselves would walk, not routes designed for tourists
cw.part1.listitem4: A story worth telling when you get home — not just another set of landmark photos
cw.part2.title: 2. What Makes It Different
cw.part2.subtitle: Side-by-Side Comparison
cw.part2.table.content1: All guides are Beijing university students or young professionals, vetted and trained by our team. Priority given to those with overseas experience or extensive background working with international visitors.
cw.part2.table.content2: Every route starts from a genuine question that international visitors actually ask. Each walk has a clear theme and a narrative arc — not a list of stops.
cw.part2.table.content3: Every route includes built-in participation moments: ordering breakfast in Mandarin, sitting with a craftsperson, stepping into a real neighbourhood shop. The emphasis is on doing, not watching.
cw.part2.table.content4: Strictly capped at 6–8 people per walk. Your guide can actually talk with you, not at you.
cw.part2.table.content5: All walks conducted in English. Some routes include bilingual reference materials.
cw.part3.title: 3. How It Works — Activity Flow
cw.part3.subtitle: What happens from start to finish
cw.part3.steps.step1.title: Before You Go
cw.part3.steps.step1.content: Confirmation email sent → Platform sends route brief and meeting point → Optional: submit personal interests for guide reference
cw.part3.steps.step2.title: Meeting Up
cw.part3.steps.step2.content: Guide meets you at the agreed location, makes introductions, runs through the day and any ground rules
cw.part3.steps.step3.title: The Walk
cw.part3.steps.step3.content: Route unfolds at a pace set by the group — there's room to linger, to go off-script, to follow a conversation wherever it leads
cw.part3.steps.step4.title: Interactive Moments
cw.part3.steps.step4.content: Each route has 1–2 fixed participation points (ordering in Mandarin, joining a craft activity). Details listed per route below.
cw.part3.steps.step5.title: Wrap-Up
cw.part3.steps.step5.content: Guide recommends nearby places to explore solo, contact details exchanged if you want to stay in touch
cw.part3.steps.step6.title: After the Walk
cw.part3.steps.step6.content: Platform sends a follow-up email with a route map and a list of everything mentioned during the day
cw.part4.title: 4. Core Routes
cw.part4.subtitle: Three routes built around different ways of understanding Beijing
cw.part4.routes.a.title: Route A  Hutong — How People Actually Live
cw.part4.routes.a.theme: Theme: What does life look like inside a Beijing hutong?
cw.part4.routes.a.description: This route doesn't take you to the postcard version of the hutong. It takes you to the one where people still live — the breakfast stall open at 7am, the elderly man who plays cards in the same spot every afternoon, the side alley that no tourist map has ever included. Walk it with a local, and you'll stop seeing it as a backdrop and start understanding it as a neighbourhood.
cw.part4.routes.a.stops.stop1: Drum Tower — Meet your guide. Brief introduction to the Drum Tower's role as the city's historical centre.
cw.part4.routes.a.stops.stop2: Shichahai breakfast stall — Interactive moment: order a soy milk and fried dough stick in Mandarin. Your guide coaches you through it.
cw.part4.routes.a.stops.stop3: A local courtyard home — Visit a guide-connected resident. See what hutong living actually looks like from inside.
cw.part4.routes.a.stops.stop4: Neighbourhood convenience store — Watch what locals buy and how they shop. A window into everyday economic life.
cw.part4.routes.a.stops.stop5: Residential alley behind Nanluoguxiang — The side streets the tourist maps don't cover.
cw.part4.routes.a.stops.stop6: Wrap-up: the corner card game — Watch how old Beijing socialises. Your guide explains what's happening and why.
cw.part4.routes.a.special: Your guide teaches you 2–3 phrases of authentic Beijing dialect — not textbook Mandarin — and you use them in real situations during the walk. Language lands differently when you actually need it.
cw.part4.routes.a.bestFor: First-time visitors / Anyone curious about real city life / Fans of street-level detail
cw.part4.routes.a.startTime: Recommend 8:00–9:00am — breakfast stalls are at their best
cw.part4.routes.b.title: Route B  A Day in the Life of a Young Beijinger
cw.part4.routes.b.theme: Theme: Where do Beijing's young people actually go?
cw.part4.routes.b.description: The day-to-day life of Chinese young people is one of the things international visitors are most curious about and least likely to access on a standard trip. This route puts you inside it — the university district where your guide grew up, the independent coffee shops they actually go to, the art spaces that aren't on any tour itinerary, and the kind of evening that ends with live music or a drink in a hutong bar.
cw.part4.routes.b.stops.stop1: Wudaokou — Beijing's university district. Your guide introduces the student culture and shares their own experience of university life here.
cw.part4.routes.b.stops.stop2: Secondhand bookshop / independent cafe nearby — A look at how young Beijingers spend their leisure time and money.
cw.part4.routes.b.stops.stop3: Caochangdi Art District — The creative community beyond 798. Fewer tourists, more working artists.
cw.part4.routes.b.stops.stop4: Evening: Livehouse near Drum Tower or a hutong bar — How a Beijing night actually ends.
cw.part4.routes.b.special: The whole walk runs like a friend showing you around, not a guide presenting to you. Along the way, the conversation naturally moves into what life is actually like for young people in China right now — what they're thinking about, what's hard, what makes them happy. No lecture. Just a conversation.
cw.part4.routes.b.bestFor: Anyone curious about contemporary Chinese youth culture / Those hoping to meet local peers / Night owls
cw.part4.routes.c.title: Route C  The Crafts That Are Almost Gone
cw.part4.routes.c.theme: Theme: What traditional skills still exist in Beijing — and for how long?
cw.part4.routes.c.description: This route is the hardest to replicate and the most memorable. We arrange access to Beijing intangible heritage craftspeople and take you directly into their workshops — not to see finished products in a display case, but to sit with the person still making them. You'll try it yourself. You'll hear their story. And you'll leave with something you made.
cw.part4.routes.c.stops.stop1: Craftsperson studio visit (pre-arranged, requires advance booking) — Choose from: Fengzheng Wei kite-making / Rabbit God clay figurines / Cloisonné enamelwork
cw.part4.routes.c.stops.stop2: Hands-on making session — Under the craftsperson's guidance, you make a piece to take home
cw.part4.routes.c.stops.stop3: The craftsperson's story — Your guide translates as the artisan talks about how they came to this work and what it's like to carry it now
cw.part4.routes.c.stops.stop4: Traditional teahouse to close — Sit with the day's experience over tea. Your guide introduces tea culture.
cw.part4.routes.c.special: Every participant leaves with something they made themselves. The craftspeople in this route talk honestly about the state of their craft — not the polished version for tourists, but the real one. This walk has a way of making people feel, for the first time, what it actually means for a tradition to disappear.
cw.part4.routes.c.bestFor: Anyone interested in traditional culture / Hands-on learners / Those wanting a meaningful souvenir
cw.part4.routes.c.startTime: Recommend 10:00am — advance booking of 7+ days required
cw.part4.routes.c.note: Please note: Route C requires a minimum of 7 days advance notice to arrange craftsperson access.
cw.part5.title: 5. Design Purpose & Philosophy
cw.part5.subtitle: Why We Designed It This Way
cw.part5.principle: We want visitors to actually enter Beijing, not just pass through it.
cw.part5.para1: Most city tour products suffer from the same structural problem: the people delivering the information are disconnected from the people who actually hold it, and visitors are placed in a passive role where they watch, listen, and photograph, but never really participate.
cw.part5.para2: We address the first problem by using peer guides who are genuinely part of the city they are showing. What they share is not researched material — it is their life. We address the second by building participation into every route. Every walk has moments where you speak, try, touch, and make something, so the experience shifts from observation to presence.
cw.part5.para3: We want every person who takes one of these walks to leave Beijing with something that cannot be photographed: a real person's name, a Mandarin phrase they actually used, and a story that belongs specifically to them.
cw.part5.note: The goal is not to collect more sights. The goal is to leave with a relationship to the city that feels lived, specific, and personal.
cw.part6.title: 6. Pricing
cw.part6.subtitle: Clear pricing for each route tier and included services
cw.part6.rows.row1.label: Introductory (2–3 hrs)
cw.part6.rows.row2.label: In-Depth (4–5 hrs)
cw.part6.rows.row3.label: Immersive (6–8 hrs)
cw.part6.rows.row4.label: Route C craft materials surcharge
cw.part6.rows.row5.label: Group discount
cw.part6.rows.row5.value: 10% off for groups of 4+, 15% off for full groups of 6+
cw.part6.rows.row6.label: Student discount
cw.part6.rows.row6.value: 10% off with valid student ID (cannot be combined with group discount)
cw.part6.rows.row7.label: Included
cw.part6.rows.row7.value: Guide for the full duration / Route reference materials / Post-walk resource email
cw.part6.rows.row8.label: Not included
cw.part6.rows.row8.value: Transport / Food and drink / Entrance fees / Craft materials (Route C)
cw.part7.title: 7. Important Information Before You Book
cw.part7.subtitle: Please read before booking
cw.part7.booking.title: Important Information
cw.part7.booking.row1.label: Group size
cw.part7.booking.row1.value: Strictly capped at 6–8 people per walk. Once full, booking closes automatically.
cw.part7.booking.row2.label: Advance booking
cw.part7.booking.row2.value: Route C requires at least 7 days advance notice. All other routes: please book at least 3 days ahead.
cw.part7.booking.row3.label: What to wear
cw.part7.booking.row3.value: Walks are conducted entirely outdoors. Wear comfortable, flat-soled shoes. Heels are not recommended.
cw.part7.booking.row4.label: Extreme weather
cw.part7.booking.row4.value: In the event of heavy rain, heavy snow, or a heat warning, the platform will notify participants in advance to reschedule or issue a full refund.
cw.part7.booking.row5.label: Punctuality
cw.part7.booking.row5.value: Please arrive on time at the meeting point. If you are more than 15 minutes late, your guide will use their discretion about whether to wait.
cw.part7.booking.row6.label: Respect for residents
cw.part7.booking.row6.value: Several routes pass through active residential areas. Please be respectful of residents and their space.
cw.part7.booking.row7.label: Route C studio visits
cw.part7.booking.row7.value: Route C studio visits are private, pre-arranged access. Please follow studio rules: no unsolicited photography, no interrupting ongoing work.
cw.part7.cancellation.title: Cancellation Policy
cw.part7.cancellation.row1.label: 72+ hours before start
cw.part7.cancellation.row2.label: 24–72 hours before start
cw.part7.cancellation.row3.label: Less than 24 hours before start
cw.part7.cancellation.row4.label: Cancelled by guide or platform
cw.part8.title: 8. How to Book
cw.part8.subtitle: Four Steps to Confirm Your Walk
cw.part8.steps.step1.title: Step 1  Choose Your Route and Tier
cw.part8.steps.step1.content: Select a route (A / B / C) and duration tier (Introductory / In-Depth / Immersive) on this page. Review the price and click Book Now.
cw.part8.steps.step2.title: Step 2  Fill in Your Details
cw.part8.steps.step2.content: Enter your name, email address, number of participants, preferred date(s), language preference, and any special requirements (dietary restrictions, mobility needs, etc.).
cw.part8.steps.step3.title: Step 3  Wait for Confirmation
cw.part8.steps.step3.content: We'll send a confirmation email within 48 hours. It includes: meeting point map, guide introduction, route overview, and a day-of checklist. Your booking is confirmed when you receive this email.
cw.part8.steps.step4.title: Step 4  Show Up and Walk
cw.part8.steps.step4.content: Meet your guide at the time and place in your confirmation email. That's it. The rest takes care of itself.
cw.part9.title: 9. Contact
cw.part9.subtitle: Get in touch for bookings or general enquiries
cw.part9.rows.row1.label: Citywalk bookings
cw.part9.rows.row2.label: General enquiries
cw.part9.rows.row3.label: Response time
cw.part9.rows.row3.value: Within 48 hours on working days (weekends and public holidays may take longer)
cw.part9.rows.row4.label: Day-of contact
cw.part9.rows.row4.value: If anything changes on the day, reply directly to your confirmation email or contact your guide
cw.part9.note: If you have specific access needs — wheelchair-accessible routes, vegetarian-friendly stops, or anything else — please note it in the booking form or email us directly. We'll do our best to accommodate.
